cataloging_subject.label: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the two-poem Middle English selection encoded in CME00080 from Max Kaluza's 1890 article: the complete texts of The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death, with textual notes embedded in the transcription but without most of Kaluza's German introductions and commentary. The poems are independent anonymous works with different witness histories; the unit is not one medieval work, the complete article, or any complete manuscript.

catalog_summary.title: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: The poems are tentatively dated by Kaluza to the fourteenth century; surviving medieval witnesses are late-fourteenth- and fifteenth-century; first published separately in 1816 and 1887; Kaluza selection published 1890
catalog_summary.region: English Midlands dialect for both poems; Three Messengers of Death is localized more specifically to the West Midlands

titles.value: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)

assertion.notes: A supplied title that makes the two-work selection boundary explicit.

scope.description: The encompassing article title; CME00080 includes only its two poem texts rather than the complete article.

titles.value: The Eremyte and the Outelawe. A Balade.

assertion.notes: Kaluza's section heading and Fillingham's designation; it is not asserted here as a rubric visible in the medieval witness. Spelling and punctuation vary across sources.

agents.name: Anonymous poet of The Hermit and the Outlaw
notes: Kaluza conjectured that the poet may have been a secular or regular cleric, but no named attribution is established.

agents.name: Anonymous poet of Three Messengers of Death
notes: The poem's learned citations and Latin verses support clerical learning but not a named author.

content_parts.label: The Hermit and the Outlaw
content_parts.summary: A religious exemplum in which an outlaw repents, accepts a seemingly small penance not to drink water, dies rather than break it, and is carried to heaven; an angel explains his salvation to his hermit brother.

locators.value: Kaluza's milestones 3a–15a; exact current recto/verso concordance unverified

notes: Kaluza treats the first 24 lines as integral although the Add MS 22577 catalog separates and miscounts them as 23 lines.
Several stanzas are incomplete and a larger textual lacuna follows Kaluza line 99; the 34 XML line groups are encoding containers, not 34 complete twelve-line stanzas.

content_parts.label: Three Messengers of Death
content_parts.summary: A macaronic verse homily presenting Chance, Sickness, and Old Age as Death's three messengers and urging confession, repentance, and preparation for judgment.

locators.value: DIV1 2; 54 LG, two Q blocks, and 220 L

notes: The XML has 54 four-line Middle English LGs (216 lines) plus two Latin Q blocks encoded as two long lines each, for 220 L total.
Cook and Strakhov instead print each Latin distich as a four-line quatrain and describe 56 quatrains / 224 lines. This is a lineation difference, not missing text in the XML.

date_statements.display: Tentatively third quarter of the fourteenth century in Kaluza's plague-based dating

assertion.notes: Kaluza inferred the range from a general plague allusion; a modern composition-date review remains needed.

date_statements.display: Surviving medieval witness: second half of the fifteenth century
Tentatively first half of the fourteenth century in Kaluza's dating

assertion.notes: The modern METS introduction confirms late-fourteenth-century witnesses but does not validate Kaluza's earlier composition estimate.

date_statements.display: Earliest surviving attestation window: late fourteenth century; Simeon is dated 1360–1399 and both Simeon and Vernon are commonly described as c. 1390–1400
notes: The normalized range combines the British Library's 1360–1399 range for Simeon with METS's c. 1390–1400 description of both witnesses; it is an attestation window, not a composition range or proof that one surviving copy is physically earliest.

assertion.notes: Park printed the prologue and narrative on pp. 91–104; Kaluza's 1890 claim that the poem was unpublished was mistaken.
Horstmann printed the Vernon version on pp. 432–434 before Kaluza's 1890 Simeon-based edition.

date_statements.display: 1890 as this exact two-poem Kaluza selection

assertion.notes: Kaluza's broad dialect conclusion; not proof of place of composition or of the fifteenth-century witness's production.
METS identifies the poem with the West Midlands language of the Vernon Manuscript; this is linguistic localization, not a demonstrated place of composition.

scope.description: Two embedded Latin distichs encoded in Q blocks

form_statements.description: Two independent verse works selected from one article; brief rubrics and embedded Latin do not change the whole-unit form.

subject_statements.label: Chance, sickness, and old age as messengers of death

summaries.value: Two anonymous Middle English religious poems as edited by Max Kaluza in 1890. The Hermit and the Outlaw narrates an outlaw's conversion, extreme fidelity to a small penance, death, and salvation. Three Messengers of Death is a macaronic verse homily in which Chance, Sickness, and Old Age warn humanity of death and judgment. CME00080 preserves the poem texts and textual annotations but omits most of the article's German editorial discussion.

The 607 XML L elements are encoding metrics: 387 in The Hermit and the Outlaw and 220 in Three Messengers. In the latter, 216 Middle English lines are grouped in 54 LGs and four long Latin lines in two Q blocks; the modern METS edition instead divides the Latin into eight short lines and reports 56 quatrains / 224 lines.

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 work containers. Part 1 has 34 LG and 387 L; part 2 has 54 LG, two Q blocks, and 220 L. The 88 LG and 607 L totals describe the encoding, not a normalized stanza or verse count across editorial systems.

evidence.citation: CME00080 XML source in the pinned CME submodule, structurally inspected 2026-07-12.
evidence.locator: Two BODY/DIV1 elements; HEAD, LG, Q, L, NOTE, PB, and MILESTONE elements
evidence.summary: Confirms the two-work boundary, titles, Middle English and Latin content, exact XML metrics, printed-page references, transcript folio milestones, incipits, explicits, and the omission of Kaluza's intervening article prose.

evidence.citation: Max Kaluza, 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften,' Englische Studien 14 (1890): 165–187.
evidence.summary: Supports the edition titles, editor, source statements, editorial practices, poem descriptions, lineation, Kaluza's tentative dating and dialect claims, and publication context.

evidence.citation: Thomas Park, ed., 'The Eremyte and the Owtelawe,' Restituta 4 (1816): 91–104.
evidence.summary: Confirms that the complete poem, including its 24-line prologue, was printed in 1816 from a Fillingham transcript.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Nachträge zu den Legenden,' section 5, Archiv 79 (1887): 432–434.
evidence.summary: Confirms the first printed Vernon version of Three Messengers of Death in 1887.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 37492.
evidence.summary: Supports The Hermit and the Outlaw at ff. 76v–82v, the witness date and physical description, the two Fillingham-derived transcript traditions, and the Park and Kaluza edition history.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript).
evidence.summary: Supports Three Messengers of Death at ff. 88vb–89ra, the late-fourteenth-century date, physical description, and current image unavailability.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 440 and 5387.
evidence.summary: Supports the two work identities, incipits, witness loci, transcript association, and edition references.

evidence.citation: Megan L. Cook and Elizaveta Strakhov, eds., John Lydgate's Dance of Death and Related Works (2019), introduction and edition of Three Messengers of Death.
evidence.summary: Supports the modern title, anonymous authorship, verse-homily and death-poetry classification, West Midlands dialect, two witnesses and loci, macaronic structure, and 56-quatrain modern lineation.

notes: The preferred whole-unit title is supplied because CME00080 has no encoded title spanning both poem divisions.
London and Oxford are holding places and Leipzig, London, and Braunschweig are publication places; none is used as a place-of-composition claim.
Kaluza's historical statements are preserved as attributed claims rather than silently modernized.

open_questions.question: What composition dates can current scholarship support for each poem beyond Kaluza's nineteenth-century conjectures?
next_steps: Consult Richard Firth Green's modern edition and current scholarship on The Hermit and the Outlaw.
Review linguistic, metrical, and source studies of Three Messengers of Death independently of witness dates.

open_questions.question: Can Kaluza's proposed but unidentified Latin or French source for The Hermit and the Outlaw be identified?
next_steps: Search modern motif, exemplum, and sermon indexes and Green's textual introduction.
Do not create a source-work lineage entity until a specific analogue and relationship are evidenced.

open_questions.question: Should corpus UI expose both the CME 220-line metric and METS 224-line lineation for Three Messengers of Death?
next_steps: Retain both attributed counts and explain that METS divides each of four Latin hexameters into paired half-lines.
